resource.note: only 1 monitored bed left — likely needed for this patient

time.note: ESI 2 should be roomed and seen immediately

warnings: Patient appearance (well, conversational) is sharply discordant with vitals — do NOT anchor on reassuring affect
Compensated shock physiology possible in young healthy adult; BP drop is a late sign
Patient wants to leave — flag AMA risk, do not allow discharge before workup

hypotheses.name: Exertional heat illness / dehydration with rhabdomyolysis
supporting_signals: tachycardia, hypotension, low-grade fever possible from exertion
rules_against: temp 38.7°C is high for pure exertional
SpO2 91% not explained by dehydration alone

hypotheses.name: Pulmonary embolism
rules_against: no chest pain or dyspnea reported

hypotheses.name: Vital sign artifact / measurement error
rules_against: multiple abnormal vitals concordant (HR, BP, RR, SpO2, T) — unlikely all artifact

immediate.action: Move to monitored bed NOW; continuous cardiac monitoring, supplemental O2 to keep SpO2 ≥94%, large-bore IV x2, draw labs (CBC, CMP, lactate, blood cultures x2, UA, β-hCG, troponin, coags, CK)
immediate.reason: Meets sepsis screen and hypoxia/hypotension red flags despite well appearance — compensated shock until proven otherwise.

immediate.action: Begin 30 mL/kg crystalloid bolus (≈2L NS/LR) and repeat full vitals + orthostatics within 10 min; physician at bedside for sepsis bundle decision
immediate.reason: SBP 88 + lactate pending; Surviving Sepsis bundle requires fluids and broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1h if sepsis confirmed.

immediate.action: Hold any discharge / AMA request until workup complete; have MD speak directly with patient about why she cannot leave
immediate.reason: Patient stated desire to go home but vitals show shock physiology; AMA in this state is high-risk.

monitor.action: Repeat vitals q5min until stable; trend HR, SBP, SpO2, RR; notify MD for any further drop in SBP <85 or SpO2 <90
monitor.reason: Pending fluid response and labs; early deterioration must be caught.

monitor.action: Await lactate, β-hCG, CBC, troponin, and chest imaging results; reassess differential (sepsis vs PE vs ectopic vs rhabdo) when back
monitor.reason: Diagnosis is undifferentiated; results will steer next bundle (antibiotics, CTA chest, OB consult, etc.).

escalate_or_redirect.action: Notify attending MD immediately; have OB/GYN and ICU on standby pending β-hCG and lactate
escalate_or_redirect.reason: Young female with undifferentiated shock — ectopic and septic shock both need rapid specialty pathways; only 1 MD on shift.

explanation.summary: 28F triaged ESI 2 (now) despite well appearance. Vitals show simultaneous hypotension (88/54), tachycardia (142), tachypnea (26), hypoxia (91%), and fever (38.7°C) — meeting sepsis screen criteria plus multiple isolated red flags. Well affect in a young patient is consistent with compensated shock, not reassurance. Differential includes sepsis, PE, ruptured ectopic, and exertional/rhabdo; all require immediate monitored-bed workup. Conservative bias applied: do not downgrade based on appearance.

citations.signal: high-risk vitals constellation in otherwise well-appearing adult
T 38.7, HR 142, RR 26, SBP 88 — ≥2 SIRS + hypotension; possible occult infection

checklist.task: Place patient in monitored bed, apply cardiac monitor + SpO2 + supplemental O2
Establish 2 large-bore IVs and draw sepsis/shock lab panel including lactate, β-hCG, blood cultures x2, troponin, CK, coags
MD bedside evaluation and decision on empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1h if sepsis confirmed
Initiate 30 mL/kg crystalloid bolus; reassess BP/HR/SpO2 after each liter
Order portable CXR; consider CTA chest if PE suspicion persists after labs
Document refusal/AMA risk discussion; ensure patient understands she cannot be discharged until stable
Reassess vitals q5min until two consecutive normal sets, then q15min
Charge nurse: confirm monitored bed assignment and protect MD bandwidth for this case

why_this_tier: Five concurrent vital sign abnormalities (BP, HR, RR, SpO2, T) — any two would already trigger ESI 2
Compensated shock in a young patient typically presents as 'looks well' until sudden decompensation
Conservative bias: discordance between appearance and vitals defaults to escalation, not reassurance
would_downgrade_if: Repeat vitals on a different, properly-sized cuff with patient seated/calm normalize completely (HR <100, SBP >110, SpO2 ≥95% RA, T <38, RR <20)
Lactate normal AND β-hCG negative AND clear benign explanation (e.g., recent albuterol use, finger-probe artifact) confirmed
Patient remains stable for 2+ hours of observation with normalized vitals
would_upgrade_if: SBP drops below 85 or fails to respond to fluid bolus
Mental status changes, new chest pain, or worsening hypoxia
β-hCG positive with any abdominal/pelvic findings → ESI 1 surgical emergency
